text: Hi there!  I represent Ditto Realty.  We have some LOVELY properties that are PROVEN bargains.  We know that's true, because we've sold them so many times.

text: You know that bears can't talk!

speaker: narrator

text: Sorry, this lock is enchanted.  You'll need a magical key.
speaker: narrator

text: As your eyes adjust from sunlight to darkness, you examine the interior of this eerie cavern.  You sense something moving off to your right.
speaker: narrator

text: A very large bear rears up as you approach.  It looks hungry and dangerous.
speaker: narrator

text: Boy, that smarts!  Your last thoughts, although trivial, gently nudge you toward infinity.  "I wonder if the bear's paws were clean.........."
say: I wonder if the bear's paws were clean...
speaker: hero

text: Boy, that smarts!  Your last thoughts, although trivial, gently loft you toward infinity.  "I wonder if the bear's paws were clean.........."

text: The Kobold's key disappears as you turn it in the lock.
speaker: narrator

text: You get the feeling that you're not alone.
speaker: narrator

text: The Brigands oblige you by making your pleasant rest permanent!
speaker: narrator

text: No spells are active here.
speaker: narrator

text: You're too busy trying not to become a human pincushion.
speaker: narrator

speaker: narrator

text: Life as an archer is not dull or pointless.
speaker: narrator

text: He has an IQ so low he can't spell IQ.
speaker: narrator

text: Sacks of grain.  Stored for the winter.
speaker: narrator

text: Empty wine barrels.  The winters here are very cold.
speaker: narrator

speaker: narrator

text: These rolled-up rugs are spares for use when the rug at the left is lost.
speaker: narrator

text: A rope tautly strung.
speaker: narrator

text: Sturdy looking bridge with one support pole across the chasm.
speaker: narrator

text: Sturdy looking bridge with two support poles across the chasm.  A message reading "Cross Here" is on this bridge.
speaker: narrator

text: Chasm across the room.  The only safe passage is across the bridges.
speaker: narrator

text: Door to some other room.
speaker: narrator

text: Window showing vague outlines and shapes.
speaker: narrator

speaker: narrator

text: Some kind of cabinet.
speaker: narrator

text: Fortification for archers to stand behind.
speaker: narrator

text: The stuff that dreams are made of.  Left here by some somnambulistic brigand.
speaker: narrator

text: This was not the ending you had in mind when you read the manual on "How to be a Hero".  Either you just aren't ready to take on this situation, or there's got to be a better way to accomplish your mission.
speaker: narrator

text: You hear a snick as the hasp on the Jack in the Box is released.
speaker: narrator

text: The "Open" spell lets you open things with a mere gesture.
speaker: erasmus

text: If you're really good at it, you can open locks.  It's useful when you lose your keys.
speaker: erasmus

text: Your keys are in the study under the skull.
speaker: fenrus

text: Why would they be there?
speaker: erasmus

text: They're skeleton keys, aren't they?
speaker: fenrus

text: "Fetch" is a useful spell for catching and retrieving small objects at a distance.
speaker: erasmus

text: It's good for putting things in their place.
speaker: erasmus

text: Like uppity familiars?
speaker: fenrus

text: I'd put you in your place, but I'm above such things.
speaker: erasmus

text: Besides, my place is above your reach.
speaker: fenrus

text: The "Trigger" spell is used to set off an existing spell, just like pulling the spell's "trigger".
speaker: erasmus

text: For example, if a chest has a magical trap on it, you can set off the trap by casting a "Trigger" spell.
speaker: erasmus

text: Just make sure you're out of range of the trap.
speaker: erasmus

text: I can set up other spells and let someone else "Trigger" them.
speaker: erasmus

text: I fixed Henry the Hermit's cave for him so that he can do alot of magic with only one spell.
speaker: erasmus

text: Now that's a spell with charm!
speaker: fenrus

text: It's true name is Erasmus' Razzle Dazzle, by the way.
speaker: erasmus

text: You cast the spell and anyone looking directly at its corruscating light is blinded for awhile and can't do anything.
speaker: erasmus

text: Magical traps are a good way to keep thieves out of your chests and other places.
speaker: erasmus

text: Put a fire trap, for example, on a chest.  When someone tries to open it, the thief will go up in smoke!
speaker: erasmus

text: Tell our hero here what happens when you forget and try to open the chest yourself.
speaker: fenrus

text: I only got singed the last time.  Nothing major.
speaker: erasmus

text:
Thus the hero from the East
Freed the man from form of beast
Saved beauty from the brigand's band
And forced the Ogress to flee the land.
speaker: narrator

text:
You have defeated the brigands
And become a true Hero of Spielburg
speaker: narrator

text:
The brigand band has been dispersed
Their treasure has been reimbursed.
speaker: narrator

text:
So with the Kattas and the Merchant
You board a magic carpet to the land of Shapeir.
speaker: narrator

text:
And so with Kattas and Abdulla Doo
You bid the valley a fond adieu.
speaker: narrator

text:
Unfortunately, since the Baron is still
cursed, and Baba Yaga remains to work her evil deeds, terror will continue to rule the land.
speaker: narrator

text: Naughty, naughty.  The Sheriff and Otto arrive on the scene and arrest you for "blatant power-gaming".  You have to *work* at it to become a *real* hero!
speaker: narrator

text: Obviously, you shouldn't have done that!
speaker: narrator

text: Now you've done it!  It's hard to be a hero when you're locked up for breaking and entering (or entering and breaking, as the case may be).  Be a little more intelligent (and a lot quieter) if you ever try something like this again.
speaker: narrator

text: Gently and stealthily, you lift the lid on the little box.
speaker: narrator

text: As the little music box begins to play, you hear the Sheriff yell out: "Otto, stop playing with that music box and GO TO BED!"
say: Otto, stop playing with that music box and GO TO BED!
speaker: sheriff

text: Boy, did YOU make a mistake!
speaker: narrator

text: Otto can't find the music box, but he's too dim and sleepy to figure it out, so he heads on back to bed
speaker: narrator

text: Otto, even in his sleepy state, winds the music box and closes the lid before he heads back to bed.
speaker: narrator

text: Otto stares sleepily at the closed music box for a moment and heads back to bed.
speaker: narrator

text: That was close! The goon must've been so dumb or sleepy or both that he didn't even see you standing there.

text: Obviously, getting in the goon's way was not one of your brighter ideas. You've had it now!

speaker: narrator

text: It must be your imagination.  What would a submarine be doing in a fantasy adventure?  Perhaps you'd rather be playing `Code Name: Iceman' -- it has a submarine.

text: You don't want to do that.  A walking pace is more appropriate for enjoying the majestic beauty of this scene.
speaker: narrator

text: Swimming in the Spiegelsee is a bad idea.  The lake is fed from mountain run-off, and the water is ice cold.
speaker: narrator

text: The game is called Dag-Nab-It.  You take turns with the Chief Thief throwing daggers at the board.  Each player gets three turns, and throws three daggers in each turn.
speaker: narrator

text: More points are scored the closer each dagger hits to the center of the board.  Highest score wins the bet.
speaker: narrator

text: Adjust force and angle of throw with the arrow keys or mouse, according to your hand position.  Press Enter or click on the small dag-nab-it board to throw.
speaker: narrator

text: Your save game disk is full. You must either use another disk or save over an existing saved game.
